planting.note: Direct sow 2-6 weeks before last frost for a spring crop; sow again in late summer for a fall harvest when flavor improves with light frost exposure. Successive sowings every 2-3 weeks extend the harvest window considerably.

directions: Scatter seeds thinly in rows 12 inches apart and cover lightly with 1/4 inch of fine soil; curled mustard germinates rapidly and can be thinned progressively, using the thinnings as baby salad greens while allowing remaining plants to develop full size. Young leaves are mild and work well fresh in salads or as a peppery sandwich green, while more mature leaves develop significant heat and are best wilted briefly in a hot pan or added to soups and stir-fries. The frilly, often purple-tinged foliage is among the most ornamental of the edible brassicas and lends visual appeal to the kitchen garden through the cool months. Avoid overhead watering late in the day, and pull plants promptly when they begin to bolt, as the leaves become very pungent and tough once the flower stalk elongates.

planting.note: Direct sow 2-4 weeks before last frost; wall rocket tolerates light freezes without damage and is best as a spring or autumn crop. It self-seeds so freely that once established in a garden it rarely needs replanting.

directions: Broadcast seeds lightly over prepared soil and press in gently without covering, as the tiny seeds need light to germinate well; thin to 4-6 inches apart once seedlings are established and harvest the outer leaves regularly to encourage continued production. Wall rocket is a wilder, more pungent relative of common arugula, with deeply lobed, almost dandelion-like leaves that carry a sharper, more sulfurous edge and pair especially well with cured meats, strong cheeses, and dressed lentils. It thrives in poor, dry, even gravelly soils where most salad greens would fail, making it useful for difficult spots in the garden. Allow a few plants to flower and set seed each season and they will self-sow prolifically, often producing volunteers in cracks in paving, at wall bases, and in disturbed soil across the garden.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before transplanting; set transplants out 2-4 weeks after last frost once nighttime temperatures stay above 50°F. Protect from late-season frost, as plants die with the first hard freeze.

directions: Start seeds indoors in individual cells 6-8 weeks before the last frost date, transplanting to the garden once soils are reliably warm and settled; downy ground cherry is a more compact, earlier-maturing species than the common tomatillo, and its small yellow fruits — each wrapped in a papery tan husk — carry a distinctive sweetness with vanilla and citrus undertones that makes them exceptional for jams, pies, salsas, and fresh eating. Fruits are ready to harvest when they fall from the plant or when the husk turns papery-tan and the fruit fills it completely. Like its relatives, this Physalis will self-sow freely in warm climates, often returning year after year from fallen fruits. Space plants generously and stake or cage them as they develop a somewhat sprawling, branching habit under productive conditions.

planting.note: Sow seeds directly in the garden around the time of last frost; maca thrives in cool, high-altitude conditions and actually benefits from frost exposure during growth. In lowland climates it performs best in cool coastal or mountainous regions.

directions: Sow seeds directly into deep, well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in a cool climate or cool season, barely covering them and keeping the seedbed consistently moist until germination; maca is a compact, low-growing brassica relative native to the high Andes above 13,000 feet, where it produces a flat, radish-like hypocotyl in colors ranging from cream and yellow to red, purple, and near-black. The edible hypocotyl has a butterscotch-malt flavor when fresh and intensifies to a deeper, richer taste when sun-dried — the traditional Andean preparation — and is used in porridges, stews, baked goods, and fermented drinks. In temperate lowland gardens, maca is challenging but achievable in cool-summer climates; gardeners in maritime regions of the Pacific Northwest, the UK, and New Zealand have had the most consistent success. Harvest when the central rosette begins to decline, usually in autumn of the planting year.

planting.note: Sow very early in spring in persistently moist or wet ground; blinks thrives in cold conditions and will often germinate through the remnants of snow cover. It is best suited to naturally wet areas rather than standard garden beds.

directions: Broadcast seeds across the surface of very moist, even waterlogged soil beside a spring, seep, stream edge, or perpetually wet bed, and do not cover them as they need light for germination; blinks is an inconspicuous but historically valued wild potherb of cool, wet habitats across Europe, Asia, and North America, producing mats of tiny, fleshy, bright-green leaves with a clean, mild, slightly succulent flavor reminiscent of purslane. Gather the young shoots and leaves by the handful for use raw in salads or cooked briefly as a light pot herb alongside other spring greens. Blinks is seldom deliberately cultivated and is far more commonly encountered as a volunteer in damp garden corners, irrigation runnels, and beside water features; the gardener's role is more to encourage and harvest it than to manage it as a conventional crop. It self-sows abundantly in suitable wet conditions and will naturalize into a permanent spring presence in any reliably wet microhabitat in the garden.

planting.note: Plant root cuttings or divisions at or just after last frost; mauka is an Andean high-altitude perennial grown as an annual in temperate climates. It performs best in cool summers and will not thrive where summer heat is prolonged.

directions: Plant root sections or divisions in deep, well-drained, fertile soil after danger of hard frost has passed, setting them 3-4 inches deep in a site that receives full sun but stays cool through summer; mauka is a striking Andean perennial in the four-o'clock family (Nyctaginaceae), producing large, somewhat fleshy leaves on multi-branched stems above a cluster of thick, parsnip-like storage roots that can reach 12-18 inches in length under ideal conditions. The roots are not edible raw — they contain calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense oral irritation — but a traditional sun-curing process of several days substantially reduces the oxalate content and transforms the flavor to a mildly sweet, starchy quality reminiscent of parsnip. In the high Andes, mauka was once a staple crop of the pre-Columbian era but is now rare even in its native range; outside of botanical gardens and specialty growers in cool-climate gardens, it is extremely uncommon in cultivation and represents a living piece of agricultural heritage worth preserving. Harvest roots in autumn after the first frost kills the tops, and save a few root sections in cool, dry storage to replant the following spring.
